articles.title: Scientists Help Mice with Sick Livers by Removing Zombie Cells
articles.summary: Scientists at UCLA found special cells called zombie cells that build up in the liver and cause damage. These cells are like broken cars that block traffic and make tissues sick. In mice with fatty liver disease from eating unhealthy food, removing these zombie cells made the liver healthier and smaller. The scientists used a drug to target only the zombie cells, and the mice got better even without changing their diet. This research shows that cleaning out these bad cells can help reverse liver problems, which might help people with similar diseases in the future.

articles.title: Scientists Find a Hidden Problem Slowing Ozone Healing
articles.summary: Scientists from MIT have discovered a hidden problem that is slowing down the healing of the ozone layer. The ozone layer protects us from the sun's harmful rays, and it was damaged by chemicals we used in the past. A treaty called the Montreal Protocol helped stop most of these bad chemicals, but there is an exception. Some chemicals are still used to make things like plastics, and a small amount escapes into the air. New research shows that more of these chemicals are leaking than we thought, and this could delay the ozone layer's recovery by about seven years. This means it might take longer for the ozone to get back to healthy levels. Scientists say we need to fix this leak to help the ozone heal faster and keep protecting our planet.

articles.title: Finding Hidden Treasure in Fool's Gold
articles.summary: Scientists have made an exciting discovery about a shiny mineral called pyrite, often called fool's gold. They found that pyrite, which is found in rocks like shale, contains a special metal called lithium. Lithium is very important because it is used in batteries for phones, electric cars, and to store energy from the sun and wind. As we need more clean energy, finding new sources of lithium is a big challenge. Usually, lithium is mined from rocks, but scientists are now looking in unexpected places. A team from West Virginia University studied old shale rocks and found lithium inside pyrite, which was a surprise because no one thought these two could be together. This could mean we have a new way to get lithium from rocks that are common, but more research is needed to see if this works in other places too.

articles.title: How Low Should Blood Pressure Go? A Simple Guide
articles.summary: Doctors are learning that lowering blood pressure more than before might help people stay healthier. Blood pressure is the force of blood pushing against your arteries. A new study used computer models to see what happens when patients aim for different blood pressure numbers. They found that aiming for a lower number, like below 120, can prevent more heart problems like heart attacks and strokes. But it also has some risks, like side effects from medicines, such as feeling dizzy or having kidney issues. The study says this lower goal is worth it for many people because it saves lives and is cost-effective. Experts remind us that not everyone should do this, and patients should talk to their doctors to decide what's best for them.

articles.title: Helping Animals in Our Neighborhoods
articles.summary: A group called Surrey Wildlife Trust is asking people to help raise money to bring back animals like hedgehogs, frogs, and toads to local areas. They want to collect £40,000 to make gardens, streets, and parks better for wildlife. This will help animals move safely to find food and homes. The trust says that because of too much building and chemicals, many animals are disappearing. They are working in places like Farnham and Guildford. By working together, we can bring back the joy of nature to our daily lives.

articles.title: Big Containers of Waste Dumped on Road
articles.summary: In Dorset, England, someone dumped large containers of liquid waste by the roadside on the A35 near Bloxworth and Morden. The liquid, which might be paint, leaked onto the road and into a drainage channel. This is called fly-tipping, which is illegal dumping of waste. Dorset Council is working to clean it up because it could harm the environment. They have set up traffic lights and closed a lane so workers can safely remove the waste. So far, less than half of the waste has been cleared. The council warns that if they cannot find who did this, the public might have to pay for the cleanup. They ask anyone with information to contact them to help solve this problem.
